Having your access to a game you bought and paid for be dictated by a volatile authentication server hosted by a third party is absurd.
regulation is needed to require publishers to have end of life plans for their software as well as a mandated expiration period for DRM, after which publishers are forced to remove it.

paragraphs.text: I appreciate the commission's concern around consumer protection in the digital space. Additionally to the practices outlined in the current version of the initiative, I would like to request the commission take a look at consumer digital ownership erasure. Over the past few decades a trend has emerged where in order to combat piracy, publishers have implemented increasingly invasive and restrictive DRM/authentication measures. These measures infringe upon consumer ownership by making it possible to remotely deactivate licenses in the case of a potential server outage, or a product reaching end of life and subsequent delisting. There already is a documented case of this happening with the Denuvo DRM server outage in November 2021, rendering many high profile games unplayable, even offline single player games. Having your access to a game you bought and paid for be dictated by a volatile authentication server hosted by a third party is absurd. The Video Game History Foundation has found that 87% of games before 2009 can no longer be bought officially. This is a direct result of industry organizations like the ESA lobbying for the inclusion of DRM and against ownership, archival and preservation of games for consumers. Additionally, a lot of recent software, particularly games, have started adopting a live service model. While there is nothing wrong with the model itself, it is being presented to consumers as a product with no stated expiration date the vast majority of the time. It is sold as a perpetual license, but the software can be shut down at any moment with often zero recourse for the consumer. This is deceptive advertising, and legally questionable. A non exhaustive list with games already affected can be found on the stop killing games wiki under the "dead game list" section. To uphold strong consumer protection in the EU, regulation is needed to require publishers to have end of life plans for their software as well as a mandated expiration period for DRM, after which publishers are forced to remove it.
